title: Death Of Ta-Te-Psin.
content:
آ آ آ آ The long winter wanes. On the wings
آ آ آ آ  of the spring come the geese and the mallards;
آ آ آ آ On the bare oak the red-robin sings,
آ آ آ آ  and the crocus peeps up on the prairies,
آ آ آ آ And the bobolink pipes, but he brings
آ آ آ آ  of the blue-eyed, brave White Chief no tidings.
آ آ آ آ With the waning of winter, alas,
آ آ آ آ  waned the life of the aged Ta-tï؟½-psin;
آ آ آ آ Ere the wild pansies peeped from the grass,
آ آ آ آ  to the Land of the Spirits he journeyed;
آ آ آ آ Like a babe in its slumber he passed,
آ آ آ آ  or the snow from the hill-tops of April;
آ آ آ آ And the dark-eyed Winona, at last,
آ آ آ آ  stood alone by the graves of her kindred.
آ آ آ آ When their myriad mouths opened the trees
آ آ آ آ  to the sweet dew of heaven and the raindrops,
آ آ آ آ And the April showers fell on the leas,
آ آ آ آ  on his mound fell the tears of Winona.
آ آ آ آ Round her drooping form gathered the years
آ آ آ آ  and the spirits unseen of her kindred,
آ آ آ آ As low, in the midst of her tears,
آ آ آ آ  at the grave of her father she chanted

آ آ آ آ E-yï؟½-tan-han e-yï؟½y-wah-kï؟½-yï؟½y!
آ آ آ آ E-yï؟½-tan-han e-yï؟½y-wah-kï؟½-yï؟½y!
آ آ آ آ E-yï؟½-tan-han e-yï؟½y-wah-kï؟½-yï؟½y!
آ آ آ آ Ma-kï؟½h kin hï؟½y-chay-dan tï؟½y-han wan-kï؟½y.
آ آ آ آ Tï؟½-way ne ktï؟½y snee e-yï؟½y-chen e-wï؟½h chï؟½y.
آ آ آ آ E-yï؟½-tan-han e-yï؟½y-wah-kï؟½-yï؟½y!
آ آ آ آ E-yï؟½-tan-han e-yï؟½y-wah-kï؟½-yï؟½y!
آ آ آ آ Ma-kï؟½h kin hï؟½y-chay-dan tï؟½y-han wan-kï؟½y.

آ آ آ آ TRANSLATION

آ آ آ آ Sore is my sorrow!
آ آ آ آ Sore is my sorrow!
آ آ آ آ Sore is my sorrow!
آ آ آ آ The earth alone lasts.
آ آ آ آ I speak as one dying;
آ آ آ آ Sore is my sorrow!
آ آ آ آ Sore is my sorrow!
آ آ آ آ The earth alone lasts.

آ آ آ آ Still hope, like a star in the night
آ آ آ آ  gleaming oft through the broken clouds somber,
آ آ آ آ Cheered the heart of Winona, and bright
آ آ آ آ  on her dreams beamed the face of the Frenchman.
آ آ آ آ As the thought of a loved one and lost,
آ آ آ آ  sad and sweet were her thoughts of the White Chief;
آ آ آ آ In the moon's mellow light, like a ghost,
آ آ آ آ  walked Winona alone by the Ha-Ha,
آ آ آ آ Ever wrapped in a dream. Far away 
آ آ آ آ  to the land of the sunrise she wandered;
آ آ آ آ On the blue-rolling Tï؟½nka-Medï؟½[BR]
آ آ آ آ  in the midst of her dreams, she beheld him 
آ آ آ آ In his white-winged canoe, like a bird,
آ آ آ آ  to the land of Dakotas returning,

آ آ آ آ And often in fancy she heard
آ آ آ آ  the dip of his oars on the river.
آ آ آ آ On the dark waters glimmered the moon,
آ آ آ آ  but she saw not the boat of the Frenchman.
آ آ آ آ On the somber night bugled the loon,
آ آ آ آ  but she heard not the song of the boatmen.
آ آ آ آ The moon waxed and waned, but the star
آ آ آ آ  of her hope never waned to the setting;
آ آ آ آ Through her tears she beheld it afar,
آ آ آ آ  like a torch on the eastern horizon.
آ آ آ آ "He will come, he is coming," she said;
آ آ آ آ  "he will come, for my White Eagle promised,"
آ آ آ آ And low to the bare earth the maid
آ آ آ آ  bent her ear for the sound of his footsteps,
آ آ آ آ "He is gone, but his voice in my ear
آ آ آ آ  still remains like the voice of the robin;
آ آ آ آ He is far, but his footsteps I hear;
آ آ آ آ  he is coming; my White Chief is coming!"
آ آ آ آ But the moon waxed and waned. Nevermore
آ آ آ آ  will the eyes of Winona behold him.
آ آ آ آ Far away on the dark, rugged shore
آ آ آ آ  of the blue Gitchee Gï؟½mee he lingers.
آ آ آ آ No tidings the rising sun brings;
آ آ آ آ  no tidings the star of the evening;
آ آ آ آ But morning and evening she sings,
آ آ آ آ  like a turtle-dove widowed and waiting:

آ آ آ آ Akï؟½ u, akï؟½ u, akï؟½ u;
آ آ آ آ Ma cï؟½ntï؟½ masï؟½eca.
آ آ آ آ Akï؟½ u, akï؟½ u, akï؟½ u;
آ آ آ آ Ma cï؟½ntï؟½ masï؟½ca.

آ آ آ آ Come again, come again, come again;
آ آ آ آ For my heart is sad.
آ آ آ آ Come again, come again, come again;
آ آ آ آ For my heart is sad.
meaning:
"Death Of Ta-Te-Psin." is a quintessential example of Hanford Lennox Gordon's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
